summary:
Athenian Democracy (508–322 BC) was the world's first known democratic political system, established by the reforms of Cleisthenes in 508–507 BC and refined over the following two centuries into a direct participatory democracy that gave adult male citizens direct power over legislation, judicial proceedings, and military decisions. Though limited to a minority of the resident population — women, slaves, and foreigners were excluded — it represented a revolutionary departure from the monarchies and aristocracies that governed virtually every other society in the ancient world.

Cleisthenes reorganized Athenian civic life around ten tribes based on geographical demes (villages and neighborhoods) rather than the old aristocratic clans, breaking the power of hereditary factions. The key institutions included the Assembly (Ekklesia), open to all adult male citizens, which debated and voted directly on laws and policies; the Council of Five Hundred (Boule), chosen by lot from the tribes, which prepared business for the Assembly; and the popular law courts (dikasteria), in which large citizen juries decided cases. The system was designed to maximize citizen participation and to frustrate any individual's attempt to monopolize power — the practice of ostracism, by which the Assembly could exile any citizen deemed too powerful, was a formal check on would-be tyrants.

The democracy reached its height under Pericles in the mid-fifth century, coinciding with the age of the Parthenon, the tragedies of Aeschylus and Sophocles, and the dominance of the [[event:1009|Delian League]]. This alignment of democratic participation with cultural and imperial greatness shaped later admiration for Athens. But the democracy also voted for the [[event:1012|Sicilian Expedition]] and condemned [[event:1024|Socrates Trial and Execution]], demonstrating that majority rule could produce catastrophic decisions as well as inspired ones.

Athenian democracy was eventually curtailed by Macedonian hegemony after the [[event:1029|Battle of Chaeronea]] in 338 BC and formally ended when Antipater imposed a property qualification for citizenship in 322 BC. Despite its limits, its influence on subsequent republican and democratic theory — from Roman republicanism to modern constitutionalism — has been immeasurable.
